keywords: bolt types, hex bolts, carriage bolts, eye bolts, U-bolts, anchor bolts, structural bolts

heading.en: Hex Bolts: The Most Common Bolt Type

body.en: Hex bolts are the most widely used <a href="/products" class="text-primary-600 hover:text-primary-800 underline underline-offset-2">fastener in construction and general applications: DIN 933 / ISO 4017: Full thread <a href="/products#bolts-nuts" class="text-primary-600 hover:text-primary-800 underline underline-offset-2">hex bolt DIN 931 / ISO 4014: Partial thread <a href="/products#bolts-nuts" class="text-primary-600 hover:text-primary-800 underline underline-offset-2">hex bolt Key features: - Six-sided head for easy wrench engagement - Can be used with standard hex <a href="/products#bolts-nuts" class="text-primary-600 hover:text-primary-800 underline underline-offset-2">nuts - Wide range of grades (4.8 to 12.9) - Available in metric and imperial sizes Common applications: - General construction - Machinery assembly - Structural connections - Equipment mounting TradeGo <a href="/products#bolts-nuts" class="text-primary-600 hover:text-primary-800 underline underline-offset-2">hex bolts are manufactured to DIN 933/ISO 4017 with full traceability and quality documentation.

bolts (also called coach <a href="/products#bolts-nuts" class="text-primary-600 hover:text-primary-800 underline underline-offset-2">bolts) feature a round head with a square collar: DIN 603: Carriage bolt with round head and square collar Key features: - Round head provides smooth, finished appearance - Square collar prevents rotation during installation - Full thread or partial thread options - Typically used with hex <a href="/products#bolts-nuts" class="text-primary-600 hover:text-primary-800 underline underline-offset-2">nuts Common applications: - Wood frame construction - Furniture assembly - Playground equipment - Agricultural structures - Where smooth head is desired on one side TradeGo carriage <a href="/products#bolts-nuts" class="text-primary-600 hover:text-primary-800 underline underline-offset-2">bolts are zinc plated or hot-dip galvanized for outdoor use.

heading.en: Eye Bolts: Lifting and Rigging Applications

body.en: bolts feature a loop (eye) at the head for attachment: DIN 580: Eye bolt (collar eye bolt) Eye <a href="/products#bolts-nuts" class="text-primary-600 hover:text-primary-800 underline underline-offset-2">bolts with continuous eye (forged) Key features: - Provides attachment point for lifting or tie-down - Various configurations: vertical lift, angular lift, shoulder eye - Load ratings clearly marked - Must be installed with matching hardware Common applications: - Lifting operations - Rigging and hoisting - Tie-down and cargo securement - Temporary attachments IMPORTANT: Always use matching <a href="/products#bolts-nuts" class="text-primary-600 hover:text-primary-800 underline underline-offset-2">nuts and <a href="/products#washers" class="text-primary-600 hover:text-primary-800 underline underline-offset-2">washers. Never exceed rated working load limit.

bolts are U-shaped with threads on both legs: Standard U-<a href="/products#bolts-nuts" class="text-primary-600 hover:text-primary-800 underline underline-offset-2">bolts for round pipe/tube Special U-<a href="/products#bolts-nuts" class="text-primary-600 hover:text-primary-800 underline underline-offset-2">bolts for square profile Heavy duty U-<a href="/products#bolts-nuts" class="text-primary-600 hover:text-primary-800 underline underline-offset-2">bolts for structural applications Key features: - Designed to clamp around cylindrical objects - Two-hole plate (typically included) for flat surface mounting - Various coatings for corrosion resistance - Often used with rubber-lined to prevent damage Common applications: - Pipe and tube mounting - Exhaust system mounting - Marine applications - Automotive suspension - Structural support for tanks and vessels TradeGo U-<a href="/products#bolts-nuts" class="text-primary-600 hover:text-primary-800 underline underline-offset-2">bolts are available in carbon steel (zinc or HDG) and stainless steel.

q.en: What are the most common bolt types used in construction?

a.en: Hex bolts (hex head bolts) are the most common, accounting for 60% of construction fastener use. They provide high clamping force and are easy to install with standard wrenches. Carriage bolts are used in wood and timber construction where a smooth finished appearance is needed.

q.en: What's the difference between Grade 8.8 and Grade 10.9 bolts?

a.en: Grade 8.8 bolts have minimum tensile strength of 800 MPa, suitable for most construction applications. Grade 10.9 bolts have 1000 MPa tensile strength and are used for high-stress applications like bridge construction and heavy machinery. Always consult structural engineer specifications for your application.

q.en: When should I use stainless steel bolts instead of carbon steel?

a.en: Use stainless steel (A2/A4 or 304/316) in coastal environments, chemical plants, food processing facilities, and applications where corrosion resistance is critical. Stainless steel costs 3-5x more than carbon steel but provides superior corrosion resistance. For inland construction in dry environments, hot-dip galvanized carbon steel bolts are typically sufficient.

q.en: What bolt length do I need for my application?

a.en: General rule: bolt length should be 2-3x the diameter for proper engagement. For a M20 bolt, minimum embedment depth is 40-60mm. Always follow the manufacturer's specifications for your specific connection design.

q.en: Can bolts be reused after removal?

a.en: Bolts should typically not be reused in critical structural connections. Thread damage, bending, or coating wear during removal can significantly reduce bolt strength. For non-structural applications, inspect carefully and replace if any damage is visible.

metaDescription: Learn about hex bolts, carriage bolts, eye bolts, U-bolts and more. Complete technical guide for industrial and construction applications in Africa.

faqItems.question: What are the main types of bolts used in construction?
faqItems.answer: The main bolt types used in construction are: Hex bolts (most common, DIN 933/ISO 4017) for general applications; Carriage bolts (DIN 603) for wood and timber; Eye bolts (DIN 580) for lifting; U-bolts for pipe and tube clamping; Anchor bolts for concrete foundations. Each type has specific applications and load ratings.

faqItems.question: How do I choose the right bolt type for my application?
faqItems.answer: Choose bolt type based on: 1) Material being fastened (wood requires carriage bolts, metal uses hex bolts); 2) Load requirements (structural uses Grade 8.8/10.9); 3) Environment (outdoor/African humidity requires HDG or stainless steel); 4) Access for installation (hex bolts need wrenches, eye bolts need loops for lifting).

faqItems.question: What is the difference between hex bolts and carriage bolts?
faqItems.answer: Hex bolts have a six-sided head for wrench engagement and can be used on both sides of a joint. Carriage bolts have a round head with a square neck that prevents rotation during installation - ideal for wood and timber where you want a smooth appearance on one side.

faqItems.question: What bolt grades are recommended for structural applications in Africa?
faqItems.answer: For structural applications in Africa's demanding environments: Grade 8.8 (800 MPa tensile) for general structures; Grade 10.9 (1000 MPa tensile) for high-load structural connections; Hot-dip galvanized (HDG) or stainless steel 316 for corrosion resistance against African humidity and coastal conditions.

faqItems.question: How do I prevent bolt corrosion in African climates?
faqItems.answer: Prevent corrosion in African climates by: 1) Using hot-dip galvanized (HDG) bolts for outdoor construction - 45-85μm zinc coating; 2) Using stainless steel 316 for mining and coastal areas with high humidity; 3) Using the correct material for the environment - avoid carbon steel without coating; 4) Regular inspection and maintenance schedule.
